findings.title: setOwner() Emits Incorrect Event — Old Owner Is Already Overwritten
findings.description: In `setOwner()`, the line `owner = newOwner` executes BEFORE `emit OwnershipTransferred(owner, newOwner)`. At emit time, `owner` already equals `newOwner`, so the event logs `(newOwner, newOwner)` instead of `(oldOwner, newOwner)`. Off-chain monitoring systems and indexers relying on this event for access control auditing will receive corrupted data, making it impossible to track the real ownership chain. This can mask unauthorized ownership takeovers.
findings.recommendation: Cache old owner before overwriting: `address oldOwner = owner; owner = newOwner; emit OwnershipTransferred(oldOwner, newOwner);`
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: initialize() Has No Re-initialization Guard — Bypasses Constructor Access Control
findings.description: The `initialized` flag is set inside `initialize()` but there is no `require(!initialized)` check. Any attacker can call `initialize(attackerAddress)` at any time to overwrite `owner`. Combined with `addAdmin` being unprotected, an attacker can: (1) call `initialize(self)` to become owner, (2) call `addAdmin(self)` to become admin (already possible), then (3) call `destroy()` to selfdestruct the contract and steal all ETH. The `initialized` variable is declared AFTER other state variables, but this doesn't prevent re-initialization since the flag is never checked.
findings.recommendation: Add `require(!initialized, 'Already initialized');` as the first line of initialize().

findings.title: tx.origin Used for Authorization - Phishing Attack Vector
findings.description: The withdrawTreasury() function uses tx.origin instead of msg.sender for authorization. tx.origin always refers to the original externally-owned account that initiated the transaction. If the legitimate owner is tricked into calling a malicious contract (PhishingAttacker), that malicious contract can call withdrawTreasury() and pass the tx.origin == owner check, while msg.sender is the malicious contract which receives the funds.
findings.message: The PhishingAttacker contract demonstrates this exact attack. If the owner calls claimReward() on PhishingAttacker, it relays the call to withdrawTreasury() with the full contract balance. The treasury is completely drained to the PhishingAttacker contract.
findings.recommendation: Replace tx.origin with msg.sender: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'); Never use tx.origin for authorization in any function that handles value.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: Missing Access Control on addAdmin - Anyone Can Become Admin
findings.description: The addAdmin() function has no authorization check. Any external address can call this function to grant themselves admin privileges. Since the destroy() function only checks admins[msg.sender], an attacker can grant themselves admin status and then call selfdestruct.
findings.message: Any attacker can add themselves as admin, then call destroy() to selfdestruct the contract sending all ETH to themselves. This enables complete fund drainage.
findings.recommendation: Add authorization: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'); or require(admins[msg.sender], 'Not admin'); Only the owner should be able to grant admin roles.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker calls addAdmin(attackerAddress)
2. admins[attackerAddress] = true
3. Attacker calls destroy()
4. selfdestruct(payable(attackerAddress)) sends all contract ETH to attacker

findings.title: Unprotected selfdestruct via Weak Admin Check
findings.description: The destroy() function calls selfdestruct with only an admin check, not an owner check. Since addAdmin() is completely unprotected (see above), any attacker can first add themselves as admin and then destroy the contract. Even if addAdmin were fixed, any admin could destroy the contract unilaterally without owner approval.
findings.message: Complete destruction of the contract and theft of all ETH held by the contract (sent to msg.sender). All user balances tracked in userBalances are permanently lost.
findings.recommendation: Restrict destroy() to only the owner: require(msg.sender == owner, 'Not owner'); Consider removing selfdestruct entirely as it's deprecated in newer EVM versions and extremely dangerous. If needed, add a timelock and multi-sig requirement.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.message: Uninitialized State Variables
findings.description: Solidity does initialize variables by default when you declare them, however it's good practice to explicitly declare an initial value. For example, if you transfer money to an address we must make sure that the address has been initialized.
findings.recommendation: Initialize all state variables in constructor
exploit_scenario: 1. Upgradeable contract's initialize() function lacks the initializer modifier.
2. The implementation contract is deployed without calling initialize() (normal for proxy patterns).
3. Attacker calls initialize() directly on the implementation, setting themselves as owner.
4. Since the implementation is the logic target of a delegatecall-based proxy, attacker now controls admin functions.
5. Attacker calls selfdestruct on the implementation (if present), bricking the proxy permanently.
6. Alternatively, attacker upgrades to a malicious implementation and drains all proxy storage.

findings.message: Sending native Eth is not protected from these functions.
findings.description: Introduce checks for `msg.sender` in the function
findings.recommendation: Add access control modifier (`onlyOwner`, `onlyRole`) or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check. Use OpenZeppelin's Ownable or AccessControl.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: Anyone Can Pause/Unpause Contract - Denial of Service
findings.description: The togglePause() function has no access control. Any address can call it to pause or unpause the contract. An attacker can permanently keep the contract paused, preventing all deposits and withdrawals. Combined with the fact that paused state blocks userWithdraw(), this effectively freezes all user funds.
findings.message: Attacker can freeze all user funds by keeping the contract in paused state. Users cannot withdraw their deposited ETH. This is a griefing/DoS attack that can be maintained indefinitely at minimal cost.
findings.recommendation: Add access control: require(msg.sender == owner || admins[msg.sender], 'Not authorized'); Only privileged accounts should be able to pause/unpause.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker calls togglePause() - contract is now paused
2. Users cannot call deposit() or userWithdraw()
3. Whenever owner unpauses, attacker immediately re-pauses
4. User funds are effectively frozen indefinitely with minimal gas cost to attacker

findings.title: Accounting Invariant Violation - Treasury Balance Can Exceed Contract ETH Balance
findings.description: When a user deposits ETH, userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value adds the full amount to user balance, AND treasuryBalance += msg.value / 10 adds 10% to treasury. This means the sum of all userBalances plus treasuryBalance = msg.value + msg.value/10 = 110% of deposited ETH. The contract only holds 100% of deposited ETH (msg.value), so total tracked balances exceed actual ETH held by 10%.
findings.message: The invariant 'sum of all user balances <= contract.balance' is violated. If all users attempt to withdraw their full balances AND the treasury is withdrawn, the contract will be insolvent by 10% of total deposits. The last users to withdraw will find insufficient ETH, causing failed withdrawals.
findings.recommendation: The fee should be deducted from userBalances: userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value * 90 / 100; treasuryBalance += msg.value / 10; This ensures the sum of all user balances + treasuryBalance == contract.balance.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User deposits 1 ETH
2. userBalances[user] = 1 ETH, treasuryBalance = 0.1 ETH
3. Total tracked = 1.1 ETH, but contract holds only 1 ETH
4. User withdraws 1 ETH successfully
5. Owner tries to withdrawTreasury(0.1 ETH) - FAILS, contract is empty
OR:
1. Multiple users deposit, treasuryBalance accumulates
2. Owner withdraws treasury (0.1 ETH per deposit)
3. Last users cannot withdraw full balances - contract is insolvent

3. Attacker calls initialize() directly on the implementation, setting themselves as owner.

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.description: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.

findings.message: AccessControlFlawed.withdrawTreasury(uint256) (../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#35-43) sends eth to arbitrary user
findings.description:
[AccessControlFlawed.withdrawTreasury(uint256)](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L35-L43) sends eth to arbitrary user
	Dangerous calls:
	- [address(msg.sender).transfer(amount)](../data/audit/AccessControlFlawed.sol#L41)
findings.recommendation: Add access control modifier (`onlyOwner`, `onlyRole`) or `require(msg.sender == owner)` check. Use OpenZeppelin's Ownable or AccessControl.
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.description: Depracated EVM Instruction for `selfdestruct` should not be used.
findings.recommendation: Add access control and consider removing selfdestruct
exploit_scenario: 1. Attacker inspects the contract ABI and identifies a privileged function lacking an access modifier.
2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.

findings.message: Uninitialized State Variables
findings.description: Solidity does initialize variables by default when you declare them, however it's good practice to explicitly declare an initial value. For example, if you transfer money to an address we must make sure that the address has been initialized.
findings.recommendation: Initialize all state variables in constructor
exploit_scenario: 1. Upgradeable contract's initialize() function lacks the initializer modifier.
2. The implementation contract is deployed without calling initialize() (normal for proxy patterns).
3. Attacker calls initialize() directly on the implementation, setting themselves as owner.
4. Since the implementation is the logic target of a delegatecall-based proxy, attacker now controls admin functions.
5. Attacker calls selfdestruct on the implementation (if present), bricking the proxy permanently.
6. Alternatively, attacker upgrades to a malicious implementation and drains all proxy storage.

findings.message: Sending native Eth is not protected from these functions.

2. Attacker calls the unprotected function (e.g. setOwner, mint, pause) directly from any EOA.
3. Without an onlyOwner or role check, the call succeeds immediately.
4. Attacker takes ownership, mints unlimited tokens, or pauses the protocol at will.
5. Legitimate users lose access or funds; attacker drains or disables the contract.

findings.title: Anyone Can Pause/Unpause Contract - Denial of Service
findings.description: The togglePause() function has no access control. Any address can call it to pause or unpause the contract. An attacker can permanently keep the contract paused, preventing all deposits and withdrawals. Combined with the fact that paused state blocks userWithdraw(), this effectively freezes all user funds.
findings.message: Attacker can freeze all user funds by keeping the contract in paused state. Users cannot withdraw their deposited ETH. This is a griefing/DoS attack that can be maintained indefinitely at minimal cost.
findings.recommendation: Add access control: require(msg.sender == owner || admins[msg.sender], 'Not authorized'); Only privileged accounts should be able to pause/unpause.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker calls togglePause() - contract is now paused
2. Users cannot call deposit() or userWithdraw()
3. Whenever owner unpauses, attacker immediately re-pauses
4. User funds are effectively frozen indefinitely with minimal gas cost to attacker

findings.title: Accounting Invariant Violation - Treasury Balance Can Exceed Contract ETH Balance
findings.description: When a user deposits ETH, userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value adds the full amount to user balance, AND treasuryBalance += msg.value / 10 adds 10% to treasury. This means the sum of all userBalances plus treasuryBalance = msg.value + msg.value/10 = 110% of deposited ETH. The contract only holds 100% of deposited ETH (msg.value), so total tracked balances exceed actual ETH held by 10%.
findings.message: The invariant 'sum of all user balances <= contract.balance' is violated. If all users attempt to withdraw their full balances AND the treasury is withdrawn, the contract will be insolvent by 10% of total deposits. The last users to withdraw will find insufficient ETH, causing failed withdrawals.
findings.recommendation: The fee should be deducted from userBalances: userBalances[msg.sender] += msg.value * 90 / 100; treasuryBalance += msg.value / 10; This ensures the sum of all user balances + treasuryBalance == contract.balance.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. User deposits 1 ETH
2. userBalances[user] = 1 ETH, treasuryBalance = 0.1 ETH
3. Total tracked = 1.1 ETH, but contract holds only 1 ETH
4. User withdraws 1 ETH successfully
5. Owner tries to withdrawTreasury(0.1 ETH) - FAILS, contract is empty
OR:
1. Multiple users deposit, treasuryBalance accumulates
2. Owner withdraws treasury (0.1 ETH per deposit)
3. Last users cannot withdraw full balances - contract is insolvent

findings.message: Unsafe ERC20 Operations should not be used
findings.description: ERC20 functions may not behave as expected. For example: return values are not always meaningful. It is recommended to use OpenZeppelin's SafeERC20 library.
findings.recommendation: Review and fix the issue according to best practices

findings.message: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.description: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.
findings.recommendation: transfer()/send() forwards only 2300 gas — will fail with contract recipients after EIP-1884. Use call{value:}() with reentrancy guard.

findings.message: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.description: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
findings.recommendation: ERC20 transfer without return value check — some tokens (USDT) don't return bool. Use SafeERC20.
exploit_scenario: 1. Contract calls an external address using .call() or .send() but does not check the boolean return value.
2. The external call silently fails (e.g. recipient is a contract that reverts, or gas stipend is exceeded).
3. Because the return value is ignored, the contract continues execution and marks the operation as successful.
4. Tokens or ETH are never delivered, but the contract's internal state (balances, flags) is updated as if they were.
5. Attacker exploits the accounting discrepancy to double-spend or claim funds they should not have.
